- **Andrej Karpathy** went viral [advocating HTML-structured LLM output](/?date=2026-05-12&category=social#item-131d519eb604) for richer information display, arguing audio-in/vision-out is optimal human-AI bandwidth
- **Ilya Sutskever** [confirmed under oath](/?date=2026-05-12&category=social#item-b83947c59b07) at the **Musk-OpenAI trial** that he witnessed Altman lying and believed firing him was appropriate
- **Soumith Chintala** [unveiled **Thinky Machines**](/?date=2026-05-12&category=social#item-b61b3d06f795) with a vision of real-time 'Interaction Models' where humans and AI speak simultaneously
- **Andrew Ng** [announced the **Coursera-Udemy** merger](/?date=2026-05-12&category=social#item-7ebce5bc5f4f), framing it around AI-era continuous learning needs
- **Clement Delangue** (HuggingFace) [showed local open-weight AI](/?date=2026-05-12&category=social#item-fe53b9ce2782) improving 2x faster than Moore's Law
- **Levelsio's** viral thread on [running Claude Code on remote VPS](/?date=2026-05-12&category=social#item-4a4b6f7d7304) captured the emerging always-on AI coding workflow shift

items.title: This works really well btw, at the end of your query ask your LLM to "structure your response as HTM...
items.content:
This works really well btw, at the end of your query ask your LLM to "structure your response as HTML", then view the generated file in your browser. I've also had some success asking the LLM to present its output as slideshows, etc.

More generally, imo audio is the human-preferred input to AIs but vision (images/animations/video) is the preferred output from them. Around a ~third of our brains are a massively parallel processor dedicated to vision, it is the 10-lane superhighway of information into brain. As AI improves, I think we'll see a progression that takes advantage:

1) raw text (hard/effortful to read)
2) markdown (bold, italic, headings, tables, a bit easier on the eyes) <-- current default
3) HTML (still procedural with underlying code, but a lot more flexibility on the graphics, layout, even interactivity) <-- early but forming new good default
...4,5,6,...
n) interactive neural videos/simulations

Imo the extrapolation (though the technology doesn't exist just yet) ends in some kind of interactive videos generated directly by a diffusion neural net. Many open questions as to how exact/procedural "Software 1.0" artifacts (e.g. interactive simulations) may be woven together with neural artifacts (diffusion grids), but generally something in the direction of the recently viral https://t.co/z21CP5iQfu

There are also improvements necessary and pending at the input. Audio nor text nor video alone are not enough, e.g. I feel a need to point/gesture to things on the screen, similar to all the things you would do with a person physically next to you and your computer screen.

TLDR The input/output mind meld between humans and AIs is ongoing and there is a lot of work to do and significant progress to be made, way before jumping all the way into neuralink-esque BCIs and all that. For what's worth exploring at the current stage, hot tip try ask for HTML.

items.title:
I'm delighted that @coursera  and @udemy have come together as one company to serve learners.

Both ...
items.content:
I'm delighted that @coursera  and @udemy have come together as one company to serve learners.

Both Coursera and Udemy were founded with the belief that access to high-quality education changes lives. Over the years, both companies have advanced this goal, creating opportunities for individuals, organizations, and communities around the world.

That role is even more important now, as AI is changing the nature of work and increasing the need for continuous learning. Helping people build job-relevant skills will be critical to how we create a better world.

By combining the strengths of both ‌companies, we can better serve this need. We bring together a broader range of learning content, trusted instructors and educators, and engaging learning experiences. This creates new opportunities to make learning more personalized, more applied, and more accessible at scale.

I’m excited to serve as Chairman of the combined company, working alongside Greg Hart and the leadership team. There is a strong foundation in both organizations, and I look forward to what the teams will build together to expand access opportunity globally.

Learn more: https://t.co/QpCwBmqWTJ

items.title: 🚨 In his testimony just now, at the Musk-OpenAI trial, Satya Nadella came off as shrewd, calm, and (...
items.content:
🚨 In his testimony just now, at the Musk-OpenAI trial, Satya Nadella came off as shrewd, calm, and (mostly) honest, an impressive leader – but also selectively blind.

How? He seemed unable to believe that the board doubted Sam’s candor, or that the board could have fired him for that reason

Yet Nadella himself testified unambiguously that would fire someone not candid to their board.

By that standard, Sam certainly should have been fired, and should no longer serve on the board.  

Witness after witness has testified that Sam lied. Toner, McCauley, Murati, and now Sutskever. All painting the same picture of Sam as a liar. 

(Karen Hao’s book, Ronan Farrow’s article, and my own Substack also pointed that way, too.)

How could Nadella have missed the signs?

items.title:
Local open-weight AI on a laptop has been improving more than twice as fast as Moore's Law!

Between...
items.content:
Local open-weight AI on a laptop has been improving more than twice as fast as Moore's Law!

Between May 2024 and May 2026, the most expensive MacBook Pro you could buy stayed at 128 GB of unified memory. The hardware ceiling barely moved. 

But the smartest open-weight model from @huggingface you could actually run on it went from a score of 10 (Llama 3 70B) to 47 (DeepSeek V4 Flash on @antirez's mixed-Q2 GGUF) on the @ArtificialAnlys Intelligence Index.

That is 4.7× in 24 months, or a doubling of intelligence every 10.7 months. Moore's Law (transistor count) doubles every 24 months. Local open-weight AI on a laptop has been improving more than twice as fast as Moore's Law, on completely unchanged hardware.
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Clement Delangue (HuggingFace CEO) shows local open-weight AI on laptops improving 2x faster than Moore's Law: intelligence doubled every 10.7 months on unchanged hardware (128GB MacBook), going from Llama 3 70B (score 10) to DeepSeek V4 Flash (score 47) in 24 months.

items.title:
I laugh when I see people in holding their laptops half open so their Claude Code doesn't shut off

...
items.content:
I laugh when I see people in holding their laptops half open so their Claude Code doesn't shut off

All my projects run on a @Hetzner_Online VPS with Claude Code installed next to the sites/apps that I work on and I just SSH in with @TermiusHQ and it keeps going forever even if I disconnect (I use Mosh or Tmux or I just /resume)

My MacBook Pro battery life is also much better as everything happens on the server not my laptop

I work so incredibly fast now, it's like having a secret benefit over everyone else who are still AI coding on a laptop, then deploying to their server, while their battery life dies and they can never close their laptop

And whenever I want I can just switch to Termius on my iPhone and continue working!

My workflow is literally: I have a bug or feature, I open Termius, I type it in the project tab, it fixes it, every fix it auto commits to GitHub but it doesn't actually deploy from there anymore because it's editing the site on the server live

I don't recommend that to everyone, but I do recommend getting a VPS you can code from and then use as staging and test and deploy from there to your production server

items.title: In this paper, a 7B language model trained with reinforcement learning learns to orchestrate larger ...
items.content:
In this paper, a 7B language model trained with reinforcement learning learns to orchestrate larger frontier models like GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, and Gemini 2.5 Pro.

It does so by writing natural-language subtasks, assigning each to one of the workers, and specifying which previous outputs that worker sees in context.

The resulting system outperforms every individual frontier model on benchmarks including GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, and AIME25, while averaging about three model calls per question—fewer than the multi-agent pipelines and self-reflection loops it beats. 

The work provides evidence that prompt engineering and pipeline design, currently done by hand in commercial AI products, can be learned end-to-end through reward signals alone.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/ihQP7Vj2Bv

PDF: https://t.co/m3ddV3s5xc
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Research paper where a 7B language model trained with RL learns to orchestrate larger frontier models (GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro) by writing natural-language subtasks. Outperforms individual frontier models on GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, AIME25 with fewer model calls.
items.reasoning: Significant technical result showing small models can effectively orchestrate larger ones through learned prompt engineering. High engagement (50K views, 449 likes). Implications for efficient multi-model systems and automated pipeline design.
items.summary_html: Research paper where a 7B language model trained with RL learns to orchestrate larger frontier models (GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro) by writing natural-language subtasks. Outperforms individual frontier models on GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, AIME25 with fewer model calls.
items.content_html:
In this paper, a 7B language model trained with reinforcement learning learns to orchestrate larger frontier models like GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, and Gemini 2.5 Pro.

It does so by writing natural-language subtasks, assigning each to one of the workers, and specifying which previous outputs that worker sees in context.

The resulting system outperforms every individual frontier model on benchmarks including GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, and AIME25, while averaging about three model calls per question—fewer than the multi-agent pipelines and self-reflection loops it beats.

The work provides evidence that prompt engineering and pipeline design, currently done by hand in commercial AI products, can be learned end-to-end through reward signals alone.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/ihQP7Vj2Bv

PDF: https://t.co/m3ddV3s5xc

items.title:
TorchLean codebase is now available!

TorchLean is a Lean 4 framework for verified neural-network so...
items.content:
TorchLean codebase is now available!

TorchLean is a Lean 4 framework for verified neural-network software. It supports typed tensors, runnable training, graph IRs, verified autograd, Float32/IEEE semantics, CROWN / IBP-style verification, certificate checking, PyTorch interop, and CUDA/GPU execution.

After feedback and comments on our original post, we expanded TorchLean substantially: neural operators/FNOs, diffusion models, GPT-style text models, GPT-2-style runs, Mamba/state-space models, RL, 3D vision certificates, Bug Zoo case studies, PyTorch interop, and more.

Project page: https://t.co/RZjTQQSGw8
Codebase: https://t.co/NfPQVz9kdu

@Robertljg, Jennifer Cruden, Will Adkisson, Xiangru Zhong, @huan_zhang12 @caltech 
#MachineLearning #ScientificComputing #Lean #FormalVerification

items.title:
Researchers just explained why giant neural networks magically work. 

Deep networks have enough cap...
items.content:
Researchers just explained why giant neural networks magically work. 

Deep networks have enough capacity to memorize pure random noise. 

Somehow they still nail predictions on data they never saw.
 
A new paper finally explains why. 

The empirical NTK quietly splits the output space in two during training.
 
Signal directions absorb gradient updates fast. 

Noise gets rotated into a hidden reservoir invisible at test time.
 
The authors turn this into a per-parameter gate above AdamW:
 
> One extra state vector
> Tracks gradient variance per weight
> Zeros updates dominated by noise
> Zero overhead at every step
 
A single batch now estimates true population risk. 

The validation set quietly becomes optional.
 
Grokking on modular division runs 5x faster. 

DPO under 30% noisy labels jumps reward accuracy from 0.566 to 0.641.
 
The same spectral filter recovers benign overfitting, double descent, and implicit bias.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: New research paper explains why deep neural networks generalize despite having capacity to memorize noise. The empirical NTK splits output space into signal and noise directions during training, enabling a per-parameter gate above AdamW that tracks gradient variance and zeros noise-dominated updates. Claims 5x faster grokking and significant DPO improvements.
items.reasoning: High-engagement technical post (80 likes, 3.5K views) covering a fundamental ML theory paper that addresses a core puzzle in deep learning. The practical implications (making validation sets optional, improving DPO alignment) are significant. Strong engagement for a technical topic.

items.content_html:
Researchers just explained why giant neural networks magically work.

Deep networks have enough capacity to memorize pure random noise.

Somehow they still nail predictions on data they never saw.

A new paper finally explains why.

The empirical NTK quietly splits the output space in two during training.

Signal directions absorb gradient updates fast.

Noise gets rotated into a hidden reservoir invisible at test time.

The authors turn this into a per-parameter gate above AdamW:

> One extra state vector

> Tracks gradient variance per weight

> Zeros updates dominated by noise

> Zero overhead at every step

A single batch now estimates true population risk.

The validation set quietly becomes optional.

Grokking on modular division runs 5x faster.

DPO under 30% noisy labels jumps reward accuracy from 0.566 to 0.641.

The same spectral filter recovers benign overfitting, double descent, and implicit bias.

items.content:
great article, mostly focused on coding agents but applies elsewhere impo. aligns w a lot of my prior thoughts:

- agents need scaffolding, not just smarter models
- failures should become durable improvements
- tools/functions should be small, reusable, and logged
- execution environments matter
- tests and deterministic checks are essential
- memory should live outside the model
- agent systems should improve through use

items.title: My latest interview with @Capgemini, sharing my view on AI sovereignty: For countries, the clearest ...
items.content:
My latest interview with @Capgemini, sharing my view on AI sovereignty: For countries, the clearest error is believing that the only two choices are to accept an American model or to build one from scratch. Those are not the right two options. The real choices are: first, to adopt a leading closed-source American platform, with full awareness of the sovereignty trade-offs involved; second, to take an open-source model and continue training it for your language, values, and regulatory requirements – at a fraction of the cost and with a realistic chance of success. Building from scratch to compete with the US and China is a third path, but it requires going in with eyes fully open to the fact that, for most countries, it is unlikely to succeed.
https://t.co/Zei7gZj8gJ
items.author: @kaifulee
items.summary: Kai-Fu Lee argues countries face three real options for AI sovereignty: adopt American closed-source, fine-tune open-source for local needs, or build from scratch (which likely fails for most)
items.reasoning: Highly credible AI leader (Kai-Fu Lee) offering nuanced strategic framework for AI sovereignty - a timely geopolitical topic. Practical advice for policymakers. Moderate engagement.

items.title: OpenAI just launched a new cybersecurity product called 'Daybreak' that pairs GPT-5.5 with Codex to ...
items.content:
OpenAI just launched a new cybersecurity product called 'Daybreak' that pairs GPT-5.5 with Codex to act as an agentic security team across a codebase.

The product scans repositories, identifies vulnerabilities, generates patches, and automates detection and response.

It ships with three access tiers, each with increasingly stricter permissions: 

- A base tier that runs standard GPT-5.5 for general security workflows
- A "Trusted Access for Cyber" tier that loosens model safeguards for defensive security operations
- A GPT-5.5-Cyber tier for specialized offensive work

Daybreak also builds a threat model directly from your repository and focuses on "realistic attack paths," reasoning about how an attacker would move through the code.

Eight security firms partnered at launch: Cloudflare, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Palo Alto Networks, Oracle, Zscaler, Akamai, and Fortinet.

Partners include Cloudflare, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Palo Alto Networks, Oracle, Zscaler, Akamai, Fortinet.

This seems like a critical reason to open up about AI use in academia.

Scholars are using old AI mo...
items.content:
This seems like a critical reason to open up about AI use in academia.

Scholars are using old AI models, badly, and not talking about it.  New models hallucinate very few citations, and good agentic harnesses drop that further. Being open about use would help us make new norms.

items.title: 1. Claude Code is not pure deep learning; without symbolic tools those results would not be achievab...

1. Claude Code is not pure deep learning; without symbolic tools those results would not be achievable
2. Mythos probably isn’t pure deep learning either
3. Software engineering is something they have spent a lot of time engineering (not just with deep learning) and it admits formal verification but its not clear we will see the same improvements in other more open-ended tasks.

items.title:
vLLM tops the Artificial Analysis leaderboard 🎉

vLLM tops @ArtificialAnlys on DeepSeek V3.2 and ran...
items.content:
vLLM tops the Artificial Analysis leaderboard 🎉

vLLM tops @ArtificialAnlys on DeepSeek V3.2 and ranks among the top deployments of MiniMax-M2.5 and Qwen 3.5 397B.

The leading deployments of these models are now open source.

How each result was built:
🔹 DeepSeek V3.2 — Aggressive op fusion across the attention path collapsed ~33 per-layer kernels down toward ~10.
🔹 MiniMax-M2.5 — Custom EAGLE3 draft trained against the target's own token distribution via TorchSpec, plus a custom QK-norm fusion for MiniMax's TP-aware attention.
🔹 Qwen 3.5 397B — Targeted fusions plus a QK-norm fix for Qwen's linear-attention path.

Every optimization is in vLLM main or on its way upstream.

Huge thank you to @inferact, @digitalocean, @nvidia, @RedHat_AI, and the vLLM community 🙏

Full breakdown 👇

https://t.co/MzxANVvhHQ

items.title:
It might not be sexy, but everyone uses authorization technology. 

Here I sit with the founder/CEO,...
items.content:
It might not be sexy, but everyone uses authorization technology. 

Here I sit with the founder/CEO, Jake Moshenko, of https://t.co/k4nTjG67Kw

This video teaches viewers why authorization—the system that determines who or what is allowed to do specific actions—is becoming one of the most critical infrastructure layers in the AI era.

While authorization has traditionally been a back-end enterprise concern, the rise of AI agents, agent swarms, autonomous tools, robotics, and eventually brain-computer interfaces makes it a frontline issue for both businesses and consumers.

The interview explains how authorization is evolving from a “boring IT function” into foundational infrastructure for the agentic future.

items.title:
Curiously the only source I can find Googling for that phrase is your own webpage!

Well that, and a...
items.content:
Curiously the only source I can find Googling for that phrase is your own webpage!

Well that, and a Google overview that points to my May 2023 interview in the New York Times Magazine (albeit with a title that doesn’t exist). But I don’t actually say that there. It’s just another hallucination. Pretty hilarious when I corrected, btw, see below; it then blames you!.

But look: by “glomming” I mean that LLMs reconstructs what are basically clusters, and it makes probabilistic guesses about sequencing those closers, modulo some analogical-like process.  I think we can agree that’s a decent first approximation.

That *process* is (excepting RL etc) pretty much what an LLM does. But that doesn’t entail *only* regurgitation; I never said any such thing (which would obviously be false).

And as someone who has repeatedly emphasized how hallucinations (which are not literal regurgitations) come out of the process, it’s ridiculous to try to pin at on me; that’s obviously not what I meant.

Is that really the best you’ve got?
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus provides detailed rebuttal to Hinton, explaining his view that LLMs reconstruct clusters probabilistically ('glomming'), which doesn't entail only regurgitation. Notes the only source for Hinton's attributed quote is Hinton's own webpage.
items.reasoning: Most detailed technical explanation in the Marcus-Hinton exchange. Articulates a specific mechanistic view of LLMs.

But look: by “glomming” I mean that LLMs reconstructs what are basically clusters, and it makes probabilistic guesses about sequencing those closers, modulo some analogical-like process. I think we can agree that’s a decent first approximation.

That *process* is (excepting RL etc) pretty much what an LLM does. But that doesn’t entail *only* regurgitation; I never said any such thing (which would obviously be false).

And as someone who has repeatedly emphasized how hallucinations (which are not literal regurgitations) come out of the process, it’s ridiculous to try to pin at on me; that’s obviously not what I meant.

items.title: If this is real, then things are getting interestinger and interestinger. We are on the verge of all...
items.content: If this is real, then things are getting interestinger and interestinger. We are on the verge of all conventional combat between advanced industrialized nations being completely outsourced to robots. What makes me worried about such a scenario is that it could *lower* the threshold for willingness of those nations to go to war. If chances of human causalities are minimal or totally eliminated, public outrage as a check on politicians' willingness to wage war would be correspondingly attenuated, even if other forms of destruction increase.

items.summary: Tunguz discusses implications of robotic warfare: outsourcing combat to robots may lower the threshold for nations to go to war since public outrage over human casualties would be eliminated

items.title:
Dear @geoffreyhinton,

 I literally never said that AI systems “JUST regurgitate”; that’s plainly fa...
items.content:
Dear @geoffreyhinton,

 I literally never said that AI systems “JUST regurgitate”; that’s plainly false. I don’t believe it, and I didn’t say it. (They do *sometimes* regurgitate, and the evidence for that is overwhelming.)

I further discuss the rest of your reply, including your alleged quote (which I can’t source outside your own webpage), and what it might mean, in a reply below.

In the best case, you have got me wrong. I certainly don’t believe what you are trying to pin me, as someone who was been warning about hallucinations (which are NOT regurgitations) since 2001.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus responds to Hinton, denying he ever said AI systems 'JUST regurgitate', clarifying he has warned about hallucinations (not regurgitations) since 2001.
items.reasoning: Central post in the Marcus-Hinton public debate about LLM capabilities. Provides his core position clearly.
items.summary_html: Marcus responds to Hinton, denying he ever said AI systems 'JUST regurgitate', clarifying he has warned about hallucinations (not regurgitations) since 2001.
items.content_html:
Dear @geoffreyhinton,

I literally never said that AI systems “JUST regurgitate”; that’s plainly false. I don’t believe it, and I didn’t say it. (They do *sometimes* regurgitate, and the evidence for that is overwhelming.)

I further discuss the rest of your reply, including your alleged quote (which I can’t source outside your own webpage), and what it might mean, in a reply below.

In the best case, you have got me wrong. I certainly don’t believe what you are trying to pin me, as someone who was been warning about hallucinations (which are NOT regurgitations) since 2001.

items.title:
/1 Developer burns a full transformer into FPGA hardware, hits 50,000 tokens/sec without a GPU.

Wha...
items.content:
/1 Developer burns a full transformer into FPGA hardware, hits 50,000 tokens/sec without a GPU.

What if an AI model ran with zero software? No Python, no GPU, no runtime. Just logic burned into a chip. That is exactly what TALOS-V2 does.

TALOS-V2 asks what happens if a small transformer is not treated as software at all. Instead of running through a runtime, it turns the entire inference path into explicit hardware: embeddings, attention, normalization, the MLP, and token sampling.

The result? Karpathy's microGPT, a 4,192-parameter character-level transformer, running at 53,000 tokens per second on a Cyclone V FPGA.

Link: https://t.co/JVVX2rn70Z
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: TALOS-V2 burns a full transformer inference pipeline directly into FPGA hardware, achieving 53,000 tokens/sec on Karpathy's microGPT without any software runtime or GPU.
items.reasoning: Technically fascinating project demonstrating hardware-only transformer inference. Though the model is tiny (4,192 params), the concept of zero-software AI inference on FPGAs is noteworthy for future hardware optimization.
items.summary_html: TALOS-V2 burns a full transformer inference pipeline directly into FPGA hardware, achieving 53,000 tokens/sec on Karpathy's microGPT without any software runtime or GPU.

items.title:
Since Hinton has actually replied let me clarify some things

- LLMS don’t *always* regurgitate
- LL...
items.content:
Since Hinton has actually replied let me clarify some things

- LLMS don’t *always* regurgitate
- LLMs don’t literally store full texts
- but given the mechanisms that they use they do sometimes regurgitate long passes through their process of resynthesis
- the evidence for that is overwhelming
- in the literature it is called “memorization”; that’s a metaphor, but it effectively works out to be something much like that.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus clarifies his position on LLM memorization: LLMs don't always regurgitate or store full texts, but sometimes reproduce long passages; this is well-documented as 'memorization' in the literature.
items.reasoning: Substantive clarification of a technical position in the Marcus-Hinton debate about LLM mechanisms.
items.summary_html: Marcus clarifies his position on LLM memorization: LLMs don't always regurgitate or store full texts, but sometimes reproduce long passages; this is well-documented as 'memorization' in the literature.
items.content_html:
Since Hinton has actually replied let me clarify some things

  • LLMS don’t *always* regurgitate
  • LLMs don’t literally store full texts
  • but given the mechanisms that they use they do sometimes regurgitate long passes through their process of resynthesis
  • the evidence for that is overwhelming
  • in the literature it is called “memorization”; that’s a metaphor, but it effectively works out to be something much like that.

items.title:
/5 Nous Research's Hermes agent now builds full HTML-native videos with HeyGen.

Your AI agent can n...
items.content:
/5 Nous Research's Hermes agent now builds full HTML-native videos with HeyGen.

Your AI agent can now make videos. No editor. No timeline scrubbing. Just a prompt.

Nous Research's Hermes Agent just got an official HyperFrames skill from HeyGen. The big idea: videos are just HTML files. Since AI agents already know HTML really well, they can now write, edit, and render full videos the same way they write code.

Why does that matter? AI agents struggle to navigate complex GUI-based video editors, but they are experts at writing HTML and JavaScript. HyperFrames skips the GUI entirely.

Link: https://t.co/qsxA6Sybr0
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Nous Research's Hermes agent now integrates with HeyGen's HyperFrames to create full videos from prompts by generating HTML/JS instead of navigating GUI video editors.
items.reasoning: Interesting agent capability development - treating video as HTML is a clever workaround for agent GUI limitations. Low engagement but novel technical approach.

items.title: Excellent book, the best one on Gödel’s Theorem(s) that I’ve ever come across. It still requires a l...
items.content:
Excellent book, the best one on Gödel’s Theorem(s) that I’ve ever come across. It still requires a lot of deep thinking to get through, and I’ve not been able to follow all the arguments in detail. However, I’m confident that this is the first book where if I DID make the effort to understand all the arguments, I could do so. One or two more detailed reading could get me there. 

One overarching point that I got from reading this book is how much reasoning behind these arguments relies on “meta” mathematical thinking, and our ability to use human language to elucidate such concepts. Those who think that the ONLY way we can truly comprehend something is if we completely formulate it in code or mathematics are deluding themselves. As are those who think that “philosophical” considerations have become completely meaningless and redundant.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz reviews a book on Gödel's Theorems, arguing that meta-mathematical thinking and human language are essential for understanding formal proofs, pushing back against those who dismiss philosophical reasoning
items.reasoning: Thoughtful reflection on the limits of formal systems with implicit relevance to AI/reasoning capabilities. High engagement (627 likes, 27K views). Tangentially relevant to debates about AI mathematical reasoning.

items.content_html:
So many companies are trying to kill the SaaS model, that for the first time I think we might have a shot at creating something different.

Literally everyone wants to replace 12 apps with a single, integrated interface that does everything we need.

AI can make this happen.

items.title:
Yes, I have several pins and glasses. 

They just don't do enough yet to get over the hump of forget...
items.content:
Yes, I have several pins and glasses. 

They just don't do enough yet to get over the hump of forgetting to put them on. And to get them to the place where they really would do everything you want you'd need to go way over the freaky line. "Hey, I see someone new, can you tell me their name and LinkedIn account?" (That's what they would need to do to make their notes and interactions really amazing, just to start).

And they bring new social problems, as you said. Think of that as a "cost" that needs to be offset by "benefit" they bring. 

Too much "cost" and not enough "benefit."

Will keep watching this genre closely because I know that someday the "cost" will be less than the "benefit." Still waiting for that day.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses wearable AI devices (pins and glasses), argues they don't do enough to overcome adoption barriers, and would need to cross 'freaky line' (e.g., identifying strangers) to be truly useful
items.reasoning: Thoughtful analysis of wearable AI cost-benefit tradeoffs from an experienced tech commentator. Discusses privacy/social friction as adoption barriers. Good framing of the fundamental tension in AI wearables.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer discusses wearable AI devices (pins and glasses), argues they don't do enough to overcome adoption barriers, and would need to cross 'freaky line' (e.g., identifying strangers) to be truly useful
items.content_html:
Yes, I have several pins and glasses.

They just don't do enough yet to get over the hump of forgetting to put them on. And to get them to the place where they really would do everything you want you'd need to go way over the freaky line. "Hey, I see someone new, can you tell me their name and LinkedIn account?" (That's what they would need to do to make their notes and interactions really amazing, just to start).

And they bring new social problems, as you said. Think of that as a "cost" that needs to be offset by "benefit" they bring.

Too much "cost" and not enough "benefit."

Will keep watching this genre closely because I know that someday the "cost" will be less than the "benefit." Still waiting for that day.

items.title:
/2 Voice-Pro dubs any YouTube video into 100+ languages locally in under 2 minutes.

Imagine taking ...
items.content:
/2 Voice-Pro dubs any YouTube video into 100+ languages locally in under 2 minutes.

Imagine taking any YouTube video and getting a fully dubbed version in another language, with the original speaker's voice, in under 2 minutes. That's exactly what Voice-Pro does, and it's completely free and open source.

Everything runs 100% locally on your machine. No API keys, no cloud costs, no data leaving your computer. You need Windows with an NVIDIA GPU (4GB VRAM minimum). Setup is just git clone, then run configure.bat and start.bat. That's it.

This opens up localized content pipelines, accessibility tooling, and multilingual apps without paying per-minute SaaS fees that typically run $23-$48 per hour of video.

Link: https://t.co/nEGWH44fzu

items.title: My car moves over for motorcyclists splitting lanes. The camera sees all. It also warns me if I am b...
items.content:
My car moves over for motorcyclists splitting lanes. The camera sees all. It also warns me if I am backing up. Actually it doesn't back up if someone is riding a bike toward the car. It can see way more than you can in a mirror. 

Cameras solve all, which is the point @Mihoda is saying.

The two cars in your picture are TOTALLY AUTONOMOUS. Can drive with no human in the car at all. And they are very safe for bikes or motorcyclists.

items.title:
The clearest analogy is self-service BI.

We democratized data.
We sped up access.
But we also creat...
items.content:
The clearest analogy is self-service BI.

We democratized data.
We sped up access.
But we also created endless KPI debates.

Why?

→ Different teams used different definitions
→ Different reports showed different numbers
→ Humans could still stop, argue, and align

AI agents do not do that naturally.

They act on whatever context they are given, or not given.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon draws analogy between self-service BI problems (inconsistent KPIs) and AI agent risks - agents act on whatever context given without arguing or aligning
items.reasoning: Insightful analogy connecting historical BI challenges to current AI agent deployment risks. Good framework for understanding enterprise AI pitfalls.

The clearest analogy is self-service BI.

But we also created endless KPI debates.

Why?

→ Humans could still stop, argue, and align

AI agents do not do that naturally.

They act on whatever context they are given, or not given.

items.title:
Most enterprise AI does not fail because the model is weak.

items.content:
Most enterprise AI does not fail because the model is weak.

It fails because the business context is missing.

That is the real bottleneck, and it gets worse as companies move from one copilot to hundreds of autonomous agents.

I unpacked this with Teresa Rojas & Tom Dejonghe from @collibra at Data Citizens on the Road in Rotterdam.

Here’s the breakdown...

if you insist on 95% accuracy even more.

the 50% is just an arbitrary criterion; there is no headroom there but so what? 

and also many software tasks more than a couple days; they just didn’t use them. so what? they can add them. it’s jsut arbitrariness on top of arbitrariness 

and the whole task is weird, as Ernest Davis and I discussed last year in my substack.

items.title:
That is why “context” is not a soft concept.

It is the operating model of the business, made usable...
items.content:
That is why “context” is not a soft concept.

It is the operating model of the business, made usable for AI.

In practice, that means:

→ What does this metric actually mean?
→ When should this rule apply?
→ Why does this policy exist?
→ What is this decision trying to optimize for?

If that is not explicit, AI starts guessing.

And guessed context is where enterprise AI breaks.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon explains context as the operating model of business made usable for AI - when context is guessed, enterprise AI breaks
items.reasoning: Clear articulation of why business context matters for AI agents. Practical framework for enterprise AI deployment.

items.title:
The big takeaway for me:

Before scaling AI, build the foundation.

→ Start with the business proble...
items.content:
The big takeaway for me:

Before scaling AI, build the foundation.

→ Start with the business problem, not the model
→ Define the process, policies, and metrics
→ Govern access to certified data products
→ Keep humans in the loop to validate and monitor

That is how you get better decisions at scale, not just faster mistakes.

Watch the full video for the full conversation with Collibra, then tell me this:

What is the biggest context gap holding back AI in your organization right now?

#CollibraPartner
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon discusses enterprise AI failure due to missing business context, advocating for building foundations before scaling AI with proper governance
items.reasoning: Practical enterprise AI advice about governance and context, relevant to current enterprise AI adoption challenges. Structured insights.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon discusses enterprise AI failure due to missing business context, advocating for building foundations before scaling AI with proper governance
items.content_html:
The big takeaway for me:

Before scaling AI, build the foundation.

→ Start with the business problem, not the model

→ Define the process, policies, and metrics

→ Govern access to certified data products

→ Keep humans in the loop to validate and monitor

That is how you get better decisions at scale, not just faster mistakes.

Watch the full video for the full conversation with Collibra, then tell me this:

What is the biggest context gap holding back AI in your organization right now?

#CollibraPartner

items.title: i mean it definitely feels like that to me - just talking to an agent, i don't do any special prompt...
items.content:
i mean it definitely feels like that to me - just talking to an agent, i don't do any special prompting, but if i have a good plan it all works well.

1. yeh fine but im thinking of how 'prompt engineering' was communicated to everyone previously - 'write your tasks like this to get the best output' → write a good plan, then just talk to the model (to get 2. done well)

2c imo falls into 'steering' - if a user knows how to steer an agent well, skills, subagents, etc.

items.summary: Ben Tossell elaborates on his view that having a good plan is the key to effective agent interaction, and that prompt engineering has evolved into plan-writing plus natural conversation with steering skills.

items.title:
/3 Open-slide lets AI agents build polished slide decks from a single prompt.

Making slides is bori...
items.content:
/3 Open-slide lets AI agents build polished slide decks from a single prompt.

Making slides is boring. open-slide lets your AI coding agent do it for you. Describe what you want, and the agent writes the React code that becomes your deck. You never touch a slide editor.

Get started in one line: $ npx @open-slide/cli init my-slide

Every slide renders on a fixed 1920x1080 canvas. Each slide is just a React component, so the agent has full creative freedom, no rigid templates to fight with.

Link: https://t.co/0Uih39gzOI

items.title:
Someone just built an AI database client that launches in 1 second. 

Most database clients are Elec...
items.content:
Someone just built an AI database client that launches in 1 second. 

Most database clients are Electron apps that hog memory and feel sluggish on a Mac.
 
TablePro is a native Mac and iPhone client built with SwiftUI. 

It launches in under one second and uses around 80MB of RAM.
 
It connects to 18 databases through native drivers, skipping JDBC entirely:
 
> MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite
> MongoDB, Redis, Oracle
> ClickHouse, BigQuery, DuckDB
> Cassandra, DynamoDB, Turso
 
The SQL editor has Vim mode, autocomplete, and multi-statement execution. 

A built-in AI assistant generates queries through Copilot, OpenAI, Claude, or Ollama.
 
It ships with an MCP server, so external tools like Cursor or Raycast can drive the client directly. 

SSH tunnels with Touch ID protection are also built in.
 
Install with one command: brew install --cask tablepro
 
The whole project is open-source.

items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI featuring TablePro - an open-source native Mac/iPhone database client with AI assistant integration, supporting 18 databases, MCP server, and launching in under 1 second
items.content_html:
Someone just built an AI database client that launches in 1 second.

Most database clients are Electron apps that hog memory and feel sluggish on a Mac.

TablePro is a native Mac and iPhone client built with SwiftUI.

It launches in under one second and uses around 80MB of RAM.

It connects to 18 databases through native drivers, skipping JDBC entirely:

> MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite

> MongoDB, Redis, Oracle

> ClickHouse, BigQuery, DuckDB

> Cassandra, DynamoDB, Turso

The SQL editor has Vim mode, autocomplete, and multi-statement execution.

A built-in AI assistant generates queries through Copilot, OpenAI, Claude, or Ollama.

It ships with an MCP server, so external tools like Cursor or Raycast can drive the client directly.

SSH tunnels with Touch ID protection are also built in.

Install with one command: brew install --cask tablepro

The whole project is open-source.

items.title:
The real opportunity is rapid experimentation.

Before:
One concept = days or weeks of production.

...
items.content:
The real opportunity is rapid experimentation.

Before:
One concept = days or weeks of production.

Now:
Teams can test 10 creative directions before lunch.

That changes how companies approach:

→ Advertising
→ Product demos
→ Storytelling
→ Training content
→ Prototyping ideas

The advantage moves to teams that can iterate fastest, not just spend the most.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.reasoning: Continuation of thread about AI creative tools. Generic but reasonable analysis of iteration speed advantage.

The advantage moves to teams that can iterate fastest, not just spend the most.

items.title: Ah yes Japan, let me check all the Japanese startups and products I use every day, oh wait there is ...
items.content:
Ah yes Japan, let me check all the Japanese startups and products I use every day, oh wait there is none 😂

My TV is LG (Korean)
My iPhone is Apple (American made in China by Taiwanese)
My startups run on Nvidia GPUs (American made in Taiwan with a machine from Netherlands)
My AI code agent is Claude Code (American)

Japan is an example how to speedrun your economy into irrelevancy, and now all it has left is tourism, cars and lenses

items.title:
What stood out to me most is not the quality.

It’s the speed.

AI video systems now let creators:

...
items.content:
What stood out to me most is not the quality.

It’s the speed.

AI video systems now let creators:

→ Turn static images into moving cinematic sequences
→ Add or remove objects with prompts
→ Instantly change lighting, style, and environments
→ Generate video + audio together

That compresses entire creative workflows into minutes.

For marketers, educators, and product teams, this is a massive shift.

i'm no scott wu, but dabbled in math competitions in middle school and took 1st in state once

but t...
items.content:
i'm no scott wu, but dabbled in math competitions in middle school and took 1st in state once

but then switched to dance in high school and ranked #1 nationally in japan w the team

and then did neither in college to run concerts and became social chair of the school

so i have no long-term focus, but good at going deep in a short amount of time (which i think makes for a good generalist VC)

items.title:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLea...
items.content:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

This is the type of application I've already built earlier at the beginning of this year for a conference demonstration! 

GPT Paper-Reader, to show how you can leverage the summarization capabilities of large language models to retrieve research papers from databases, summarize their content, and engage in Q&A interactions with people.    The future is here.  In addition to the regular theoretical presentation, I'll be presenting a practical GPT. This application utilizes GPT, to effectively read and analyze complete academic papers:  Basically the app chunks the PDF paper into manageable sections for detailed reading and generates concise summaries for each segment. By maintaining context from previous sections within the token limit, it enhances comprehension. Before diving into the paper, you can outline specific questions in the prompt. This approach allows GPT to extract the most important information during its reading and summarizing process, leading to superior outcomes.   After summarizing all parts, you will receive comprehensive answers to your inquiries based on the consolidated summaries. By default, the initialized prompt will target essential points such as:  These inquiries are tailored for research articles within the computer science domain. Upon completion of the paper review, feel free to engage with the question() interface to ask further questions.

References 

Khan, A. A., Hasan, M. T., Kemell, K. K., Rasku, J., & Abrahamsson, P. (2025). Developing retrieval augmented generation (RAG) based LLM systems from PDFs: An experience report [Preprint]. arXiv. Retrieved April 3, 2025, from https://t.co/YCGfBCNY4v

Santos, G. (2025, March 3). LLM + RAG: Creating an AI-powered file reader assistant. Towards Data Science. Retrieved April 3, 2025, from https://t.co/YubIqDgyP8

items.title:
I don't like the power cable that comes with the Framework 13 Pro.

It's one of those 3-piece cables...
items.content:
I don't like the power cable that comes with the Framework 13 Pro.

It's one of those 3-piece cables. Thick, inflexible, ugly cable that takes a ton of space in your backpack and you really don't want to take out anywhere.

(The laptop has an infinite battery life, so this is less of a problem, but still).

I bought an Anker charger, and now I'm convinced this is the best charger I've ever seen and used.

I'm using this charger now even with my MacBook Air. I thought Apple's charger with magsafe was great, but this is better.

items.content:
My noise canceling headphones are Sony WH 5's and they're TERRIBLE

The software destroyed them

Everytime I swallow or even just as much as sniff the voice to talk goes on, you can switch it off but only once

Everytime I switch the connection to my laptop or phone it resets that again

Japan's software is some of the worst on Earth

items.content:
GaN - GA - Gallium Nitride Processor. Ft.@gp_pulipaka by @Analyticsindiam 

This is how Gallium looks like.  The process of invention dates back to the year, 1874.  An ancient periodic element from the periodic table that we need to check. 

It has an atomic mass of 70 u with a melting point at 85.57 F. Gallium can form a number of compounds. Chief among them are: Gallium arsenide (GaA), Gallium nitride (GaN), and Gallium phosphide (GP).

items.content:
It's mostly culturally relevant for American and Brazilian manchildren

Korean now and soon Chinese cultural exports are way more dominant worldwide than Japanese

Kids these days watch KPop Demon Hunters, barely any kids watch Japanese stuff, that's mostly 30+ manchildren

Americans especially have a blindspot with Asia and the only place they visit is Japan usually as the rest of Asia is too scary for them

K-Pop passed by Anime/Manga in revenue $ too btw
